rejected_findings.source_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net sixty (60) days from the date of invoice.
rejected_findings.proposed_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.
rejected_findings.external_comment: We would like to align the payment term with our standard AP cycle of Net 30, which is market-standard for SaaS subscriptions of this nature and will reduce administrative complexity on both sides.
rejected_findings.materiality_rationale: Net-60 terms extend cash conversion by 30 days beyond Customer's standard AP cycle and create internal budget variance tracking that is not offset by material discounting or other deal concessions; this misalignment imposes process cost without corresponding benefit.
rejected_findings.fallback: Net-45 if Lattice offers a discount of at least 2% or if Customer determines the Subscription Services are critical-path and communicates that determination in writing to Lattice.
rejected_findings.suggested_text: All invoices are payable in U.S. dollars and are due net thirty (30) days from the date of invoice.
rejected_findings.rejection_reason: Proposed Net 30 shortens payment timing from Net 60; Customer-side reviews must not decrease Net-term days.

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 4.4(b): 'if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term' CROSS-REFERENCED WITH Section 4.2: 'upon expiration of the Initial Term, the Subscription Services will automatically renew for successive one-year periods... unless either Party provides written notice of non-renewal to the other Party at least sixty (60) days prior to the end of the then-current Subscription Term'
auditor_findings.external_comment: CROSS-SECTION HAZARD: The combination of the 60-day auto-renewal notice requirement and the accelerated-payment-on-early-termination provision creates a compounding trap. If Customer misses the 60-day non-renewal window by even one day, Customer is locked into a full additional one-year Renewal Term. If Customer then attempts to terminate mid-Renewal-Term due to budget constraints, acquisition, or vendor underperformance that does not rise to 'material breach,' Customer must pay 100% of the remaining Renewal Term fees with zero service delivery. In a multi-year relationship, this construct eliminates Customer's exit optionality and creates a sunk-cost penalty that senior counsel would flag as commercially unreasonable and potentially unenforceable as a disguised penalty. The termination-remedies-analyst correctly flagged finding 004 as blocker severity on the accelerated-payment provision in isolation, but did not capture the multiplicative hazard when combined with the auto-renewal mechanics.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Individual specialists looked at auto-renewal notice (finding termination-remedies-analyst-001) and accelerated payment on early termination (finding termination-remedies-analyst-004) separately. Each was correctly flagged. However, the COMBINATION creates an existential hazard: a single missed 60-day deadline locks Customer into a full year, and any attempt to exit that year triggers 100% payment acceleration. In a $500K ARR deal, missing the notice deadline by one day creates a $500K liability with no corresponding service value. This is a vendor lock-in mechanism disguised as standard renewal + termination language, and it is the combination—not either provision alone—that makes it existential.
auditor_findings.position: Delete the accelerated-payment provision entirely (per termination-remedies-analyst-004 position) AND reduce the non-renewal notice to 30 days (per termination-remedies-analyst-001 position). The combination of these two fixes eliminates the compounding trap.
auditor_findings.fallback: If Lattice refuses to delete accelerated payment: (1) Reduce non-renewal notice to 30 days AND (2) cap any early-termination liability at three months of fees, payable only after the first 12 months of the Initial Term.
auditor_findings.walkaway: Any construct that combines auto-renewal notice >60 days with accelerated payment exceeding six months of fees. The Profile flags >90-day notice as a deal-breaker; the combination of 60-day notice + 100% acceleration is functionally equivalent to a >90-day notice regime because the economic penalty for missing the deadline is prohibitive.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1). Delaware courts scrutinize liquidated damages provisions disguised as payment terms and disfavor penalties. The combination here may be challenged as an unenforceable penalty if the accelerated payment materially exceeds Lattice's actual damages from early termination (which in a SaaS context are typically limited to lost margin, not gross revenue).

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 9.1: 'Lattice may engage third parties to perform any of its obligations under this Agreement, including without limitation hosting, content delivery, support, and processing of Customer Data. Lattice will remain responsible for the performance of any such subcontractors... Lattice is not required to provide advance notice to Customer of, or obtain Customer's consent to, the engagement, replacement, or termination of any subcontractor.' CROSS-REFERENCED WITH Section 5.2: 'Lattice will implement and maintain a written information security program... Lattice will maintain a SOC 2 Type II report...' AND Section 5.3: 'Lattice will notify Customer in writing... within seventy-two (72) hours, after becoming aware of any unauthorized access to or acquisition of Customer Data'
auditor_findings.external_comment: CROSS-SECTION HAZARD: Section 9.1 grants Lattice unilateral, no-notice subcontracting rights, including for 'processing of Customer Data.' Section 5.2 requires Lattice to maintain a SOC 2 Type II report and information security program, but does not require flowdown of these obligations to subcontractors. The Profile requires 'Notice required + customer approval for material subcontractors (defined as those processing customer data or providing >25% of services)' and 'Vendor flowdown of obligations is required.' No specialist flagged this because performance-obligations-analyst does not exist in this pipeline (construction/services-focused specialist not run for SaaS deal), and data-security obligations were reviewed by risk-allocation-analyst in isolation without analyzing the subcontracting cross-section. If Lattice engages a subcontractor to process Customer Data and that subcontractor lacks equivalent security controls, Customer has no advance notice, no approval right, and no contractual assurance that the subcontractor is bound by the same SOC 2 / security obligations that bind Lattice.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: Specialists reviewed subcontracting mechanics (absent from coverage_pass, indicating no specialist explicitly covered it as a checklist item) and data security obligations separately. The HAZARD emerges at the intersection: Lattice can introduce a data-processing subcontractor with zero notice and zero flowdown obligation, creating a security gap. In a SaaS engagement where Customer Data includes operational telemetry and potentially sensitive business information, uncontrolled subcontractor access is a material data-breach vector. The Profile explicitly requires notice + approval for material subcontractors and flowdown of obligations, but no specialist flagged the absence because it requires seeing both Section 9.1 (subcontracting) and Section 5.2 (security obligations) simultaneously and recognizing that one undermines the other.
auditor_findings.position: Amend Section 9.1 to require: (a) advance written notice to Customer of any subcontractor engaged to process Customer Data or provide >25% of Subscription Services; (b) Customer approval (not to be unreasonably withheld) for such material subcontractors; (c) contractual flowdown to all subcontractors of Lattice's obligations under Sections 5 (Data Security; Privacy) and 7 (Confidentiality). Add to Section 9.1: 'Lattice shall ensure that any subcontractor processing Customer Data is bound by confidentiality and data security obligations no less protective than those set forth in this Agreement, and Lattice shall remain fully liable for any acts or omissions of its subcontractors.'
auditor_findings.fallback: Lattice provides 30 days' advance notice of material subcontractors (processing Customer Data or >25% of services) with Customer retaining a right to object for reasonable data-security or compliance concerns; flowdown of Sections 5 and 7 obligations is mandatory and Lattice warrants compliance.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1). Multi-jurisdictional data protection laws (GDPR, CCPA, sector-specific regulations) impose data-controller responsibilities on Customer regardless of subprocessor arrangements; Customer's inability to audit or approve subcontractors creates compliance exposure.

auditor_findings.source_text: Section 8.6: 'EXCEPT FOR (A) AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM, (B) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), AND (C) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY), IN NO EVENT WILL EITHER PARTY'S TOTAL CUMULATIVE LIABILITY ARISING OUT OF OR RELATED TO THIS AGREEMENT EXCEED THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE LIABILITY.'
auditor_findings.external_comment: EXISTENTIAL ESCALATION: The risk-allocation-analyst correctly identified that the liability cap lacks a carve-out for data breach involving personal data (finding risk-allocation-analyst-001, severity major, existential false) and gross negligence/willful misconduct (finding risk-allocation-analyst-002, severity major, existential false). However, the analyst undershot the existential character of these omissions when viewed in the full risk context of this deal. Customer is a software/SaaS company engaging a telemetry platform vendor to process operational data (which the Profile assumes may include personal data, given compliance_data_privacy module enabled). A Security Incident (defined in Section 5.3) involving unauthorized access to Customer Data containing personal data triggers: (1) regulatory notification obligations under state breach laws and potentially GDPR; (2) potential regulatory fines; (3) credit monitoring and notification costs; (4) reputational harm and customer churn. In the absence of a super-cap, Customer's recovery from Lattice is capped at 1x trailing fees even if the breach costs exceed that amount by an order of magnitude. In a $500K ARR deal, Customer's cap is $500K, but breach remediation costs (notification, monitoring, legal defense, regulatory response) routinely exceed $1-2M for mid-market companies. The combination of capped liability + no gross negligence carve-out means that if Lattice KNOWINGLY fails to patch a critical vulnerability or INTENTIONALLY misuses Customer Data, Customer's recovery is still capped at 1x fees. This is existential because it eliminates Customer's ability to recover for the most severe category of vendor misconduct at a time when Customer's own regulatory and financial exposure is uncapped.
auditor_findings.materiality_rationale: The risk-allocation-analyst reviewed the liability cap and correctly identified the missing carve-outs as 'major' severity findings. However, the analyst evaluated each carve-out in isolation and marked existential:false for both. The EXISTENTIAL character emerges when you layer the missing carve-outs onto the actual risk profile: (1) Customer is in software/SaaS (compliance_data_privacy enabled in Profile); (2) Lattice processes Customer Data, which in a telemetry context includes operational logs that may contain user identifiers, IP addresses, session data, or other personal data; (3) Section 5.3 defines Security Incidents but caps Lattice's liability at 1x fees; (4) Customer's own liability to its end-users, regulators, and partners is UNCAPPED under applicable data-breach laws. The gap between Customer's uncapped exposure and Lattice's capped liability, COMBINED with the absence of a gross negligence carve-out, creates an existential misalignment: Lattice can act recklessly (gross negligence) or even deliberately (willful misconduct) and Customer's recovery is capped. Senior counsel would flag this as a deal-killing issue in any agreement involving personal data processing.
auditor_findings.position: Add both carve-outs: (D) super-cap for Security Incidents involving personal data at 3x trailing 12-month fees or insurance coverage limits, whichever is higher (per risk-allocation-analyst-001 position); AND (E) uncapped liability for gross negligence, willful misconduct, and fraud by either party (per risk-allocation-analyst-002 position). Both are mandatory.
auditor_findings.fallback: Super-cap for data breach at 2x trailing fees (per risk-allocation-analyst-001 fallback) AND carve-out limited to willful misconduct and fraud only, excluding gross negligence (per risk-allocation-analyst-002 fallback). Customer would accept this fallback ONLY if Lattice provides evidence of $10M+ cyber liability insurance with Customer named as additional insured.
auditor_findings.walkaway: Refusal to add ANY super-cap for data breach involving personal data, OR refusal to carve out willful misconduct from the liability cap. The Profile lists 'No data-breach notification timeline' as a deal-breaker; the logical corollary is that data-breach LIABILITY must be addressed with adequate cap treatment. A 1x-fees cap for data breach is functionally equivalent to no remedy in the event of a material incident.
auditor_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1). Multi-jurisdictional: data breach notification laws vary by state; GDPR applies if Customer has EU data subjects; sector-specific regulations (GLBA, HIPAA, though not indicated in Profile) may impose additional requirements. Customer's uncapped regulatory exposure makes the capped vendor liability existential.

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 8.6 exceptions: '(A) AMOUNTS OWED UNDER ANY ORDER FORM, (B) A PARTY'S BREACH OF SECTION 7 (CONFIDENTIALITY), AND (C) LATTICE'S OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.3 (LATTICE IP INDEMNITY)' in conjunction with proposed mutual confidentiality indemnity in new Section 8.8 and proposed insertion of Customer additional-insured status on E&O policy
coherence_findings.proposed_text: Confirm that the proposed new Section 8.8 (Mutual Confidentiality Indemnity) is intended to be CARVED OUT of the Section 8.6 liability cap, consistent with the existing carve-out for confidentiality breach in Section 8.6(B). If the parties intend Section 8.8 indemnity obligations to be uncapped (or subject to a higher cap), Section 8.6 should be amended to add: '(D) EITHER PARTY'S INDEMNITY OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.8 (MUTUAL CONFIDENTIALITY INDEMNITY).' Without this explicit carve-out, there is ambiguity whether third-party indemnification for confidentiality breach is uncapped (as the direct breach liability is) or subject to the general cap.
coherence_findings.external_comment: The contract carves out direct liability for confidentiality breach (Section 8.6(B)) from the liability cap, but the proposed new Section 8.8 creates indemnification obligations for third-party claims arising from confidentiality breach. The interaction between these provisions is unclear: if Lattice breaches Section 7 and Customer faces a third-party claim, is Lattice's Section 8.8 indemnity obligation capped at 1x fees (under Section 8.6 general cap) or uncapped (consistent with the Section 8.6(B) carve-out for confidentiality breach)? The confidentiality indemnity finding should clarify the intended cap treatment to avoid post-signature disputes.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: If the Section 8.8 indemnity is subject to the general 1x-fees cap while direct confidentiality-breach liability is uncapped, the contract creates an internal contradiction: Customer would have uncapped recovery for its own damages from a Lattice confidentiality breach but capped recovery when defending a third-party claim arising from the same breach. This asymmetry makes no commercial sense and creates litigation risk. The coherence issue must be resolved before signature to ensure the parties have a meeting of the minds on the scope of confidentiality-related liability.
coherence_findings.position: Add explicit carve-out in Section 8.6 for Section 8.8 indemnity obligations to ensure consistency with the confidentiality-breach carve-out.
coherence_findings.fallback: Annotate the ambiguity and require the parties to clarify cap treatment during negotiation, with fallback that Section 8.8 indemnity is subject to a super-cap (e.g., 2x or 3x fees) if not fully uncapped.
coherence_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1). Ambiguous liability-cap provisions are construed against the drafter (Lattice).
coherence_findings.suggested_text: Confirm that the proposed new Section 8.8 (Mutual Confidentiality Indemnity) is intended to be CARVED OUT of the Section 8.6 liability cap, consistent with the existing carve-out for confidentiality breach in Section 8.6(B). If the parties intend Section 8.8 indemnity obligations to be uncapped (or subject to a higher cap), Section 8.6 should be amended to add: '(D) EITHER PARTY'S INDEMNITY OBLIGATIONS UNDER SECTION 8.8 (MUTUAL CONFIDENTIALITY INDEMNITY).' Without this explicit carve-out, there is ambiguity whether third-party indemnification for confidentiality breach is uncapped (as the direct breach liability is) or subject to the general cap.

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 9.2(b) as proposed to be amended: 'errors and omissions / professional liability insurance with limits of not less than five million U.S. dollars ($5,000,000) per occurrence and in the aggregate, which insurance shall include coverage for cyber liability and technology errors and omissions' AND proposed insertion requiring Customer to be named as additional insured on E&O policy
coherence_findings.proposed_text: Confirm that the additional-insured endorsement on the E&O policy will extend to the cyber-liability component of the coverage. Many E&O policies with cyber coverage as a rider or sublimit exclude additional-insured status from the cyber component. The proposed amendment to Section 9.2(b) mandating cyber coverage and the proposed additional-insured insertion should be coordinated to ensure Customer's additional-insured status applies to both E&O and cyber risks.
coherence_findings.external_comment: The insurance-coverage analyst has proposed (1) mandatory cyber-liability inclusion in the E&O policy and (2) Customer as additional insured on the E&O policy. These two edits must be coordinated: if the cyber component is subject to a sublimit or separate rider that excludes additional-insured status, Customer will lack coverage for the most material risk category (data breach and cyber incidents). The contract should clarify that additional-insured status extends to all components of the E&O policy, including cyber.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: If Customer is named as additional insured only on the technology-E&O component but not the cyber-liability component, Customer has no direct insurance recourse for Security Incidents under Section 5.3 or data-breach claims—the very risks the cyber-coverage mandate is intended to address. This gap would defeat the purpose of the additional-insured requirement and leave Customer relying on Lattice to tender and pursue cyber claims, creating the dependency risk the additional-insured status is meant to eliminate.
coherence_findings.position: Amend the additional-insured insertion to specify: 'Customer shall be named as an additional insured on Lattice's errors and omissions / professional liability insurance policy, including any cyber liability coverage component thereof, with respect to claims arising from Lattice's performance under this Agreement.'
coherence_findings.fallback: Require Lattice to provide a copy of the policy or endorsement evidencing that additional-insured status extends to cyber coverage, or accept separate cyber policy with Customer as additional insured.
coherence_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law). Additional-insured mechanics are governed by the insurance policy and applicable state insurance regulations.
coherence_findings.suggested_text: Confirm that the additional-insured endorsement on the E&O policy will extend to the cyber-liability component of the coverage. Many E&O policies with cyber coverage as a rider or sublimit exclude additional-insured status from the cyber component. The proposed amendment to Section 9.2(b) mandating cyber coverage and the proposed additional-insured insertion should be coordinated to ensure Customer's additional-insured status applies to both E&O and cyber risks.

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 5.5 as proposed to be amended: 'Lattice will treat aggregated Usage Data that, despite anonymization, reveals commercially sensitive information about Customer's operations or usage patterns as Customer Confidential Information subject to Section 7' AND Section 7.4 as proposed to be amended: confidentiality tail extended from 3 years to 5 years
coherence_findings.proposed_text: Confirm that the extended 5-year confidentiality tail applies to aggregated Usage Data that is deemed Customer Confidential Information under the proposed Section 5.5 amendment. The interaction between these two amendments should be explicit: if aggregated Usage Data revealing Customer's operational patterns is Confidential Information, it should be subject to the 5-year tail, not released after 3 years.
coherence_findings.external_comment: The protective-provisions analyst has proposed both (1) extending the confidentiality tail to 5 years and (2) treating certain aggregated Usage Data as Customer Confidential Information when it reveals commercially sensitive Customer-specific patterns. These two edits interact: if the Usage Data deemed confidential is subject only to the original 3-year tail, Lattice could disclose Customer's operational fingerprint in year 4, defeating the purpose of the confidentiality classification. The contract should clarify that all Customer Confidential Information—including Usage Data classified as such under Section 5.5—is subject to the 5-year tail.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: In a telemetry and analytics platform, Customer's usage patterns and operational scale are among the most commercially sensitive disclosures. If Lattice is permitted to disclose such information after 3 years but other Confidential Information is protected for 5 years, Customer faces asymmetric protection for what may be its most sensitive data. The incoherence creates a loophole that Lattice could exploit by arguing Usage Data (even if classified as Confidential Information) is subject to a shorter tail.
coherence_findings.position: Amend Section 7.4 to clarify: 'The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of five (5) years thereafter, except that with respect to trade secrets such obligations will continue for as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret under applicable law. For clarity, this five-year tail applies to all Confidential Information, including aggregated Usage Data deemed Confidential Information under Section 5.5.'
coherence_findings.fallback: Annotate the ambiguity and require clarification during negotiation; accept 4-year tail for Usage Data if Lattice resists parity.
coherence_findings.suggested_text: Confirm that the extended 5-year confidentiality tail applies to aggregated Usage Data that is deemed Customer Confidential Information under the proposed Section 5.5 amendment. The interaction between these two amendments should be explicit: if aggregated Usage Data revealing Customer's operational patterns is Confidential Information, it should be subject to the 5-year tail, not released after 3 years.

coherence_findings.source_text: Section 6.1: 'Lattice and its licensors own and shall retain all right, title, and interest in and to the Subscription Services, the Documentation, and any improvements, enhancements, derivative works, modifications, or other developments to or based on any of the foregoing' AND proposed new Section 6.4 (Customer-Specific Outputs): 'jointly owned by the Parties'
coherence_findings.proposed_text: Clarify the boundary between Section 6.1 (Lattice ownership of platform improvements and derivative works) and proposed Section 6.4 (joint ownership of Customer-Specific Outputs). If a Customer-Specific Output constitutes an 'improvement, enhancement, or derivative work' to the Subscription Services, does Section 6.1 or Section 6.4 control? The contract should specify that Customer-Specific Outputs are carved out of Section 6.1's broad sweep, or define 'improvement' in Section 6.1 to exclude Customer-Specific Outputs.
coherence_findings.external_comment: Section 6.1 grants Lattice ownership of 'any improvements, enhancements, derivative works, modifications, or other developments to or based on' the Subscription Services. The proposed Section 6.4 creates joint ownership of Customer-Specific Outputs (reports, dashboards, analytics). There is potential overlap: if Lattice incorporates a Customer-Specific Output (e.g., a dashboard) into the general Subscription Services, does it become a Lattice-owned 'improvement' under Section 6.1, or does it remain jointly owned under Section 6.4? Without clarification, the two provisions create ambiguous ownership when Customer-Specific Outputs are integrated into the platform.
coherence_findings.materiality_rationale: If Lattice can claim exclusive ownership of Customer-Specific Outputs by incorporating them into the Subscription Services as 'improvements,' the joint-ownership protection in Section 6.4 is illusory. Customer loses control over dashboards, reports, and analytics it commissioned and paid for. The ambiguity invites post-signature disputes over whether specific outputs are 'platform improvements' (Lattice-owned) or 'Customer-Specific Outputs' (jointly owned).
coherence_findings.position: Amend Section 6.1 to add: 'For clarity, "improvements, enhancements, derivative works, modifications, or other developments" under this Section 6.1 do not include Customer-Specific Outputs as defined in Section 6.4, which are governed solely by Section 6.4.' Alternatively, amend Section 6.4 to add: 'Notwithstanding Section 6.1, Customer-Specific Outputs shall be jointly owned as set forth in this Section 6.4 and shall not be deemed improvements or derivative works to the Subscription Services for purposes of Section 6.1.'
coherence_findings.fallback: Annotate the ambiguity and require clarification during negotiation; accept Customer-Specific Outputs being Lattice-owned if Lattice grants Customer a perpetual, irrevocable, royalty-free license to use them.
coherence_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1). Delaware law on joint ownership and derivative works is fact-specific.
coherence_findings.suggested_text: Clarify the boundary between Section 6.1 (Lattice ownership of platform improvements and derivative works) and proposed Section 6.4 (joint ownership of Customer-Specific Outputs). If a Customer-Specific Output constitutes an 'improvement, enhancement, or derivative work' to the Subscription Services, does Section 6.1 or Section 6.4 control? The contract should specify that Customer-Specific Outputs are carved out of Section 6.1's broad sweep, or define 'improvement' in Section 6.1 to exclude Customer-Specific Outputs.

restored_findings.proposed_text: Add to Section 8.6 exceptions: '(D) LATTICE'S LIABILITY FOR ANY SECURITY INCIDENT UNDER SECTION 5.3 INVOLVING UNAUTHORIZED ACCESS TO OR ACQUISITION OF CUSTOMER DATA CONTAINING PERSONAL DATA, PROVIDED THAT SUCH LIABILITY SHALL NOT EXCEED THREE (3) TIMES THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE SECURITY INCIDENT.'
restored_findings.external_comment: We require a super-cap for Security Incidents involving personal data. Given the regulatory exposure and notification costs associated with data breaches, the general liability cap does not adequately address this category of risk. A 3x super-cap is consistent with market practice for SaaS agreements handling customer operational data.
restored_findings.materiality_rationale: RESTORATION RATIONALE: The compiler rejected this finding on posture-integrity grounds, but the rejection was incorrect. The proposed edit does NOT move the contract against Customer. It adds a LIMITED super-cap (3x fees) for a specific category of harm (Security Incidents involving personal data), which is a CUSTOMER-FAVORABLE position that is entirely appropriate for a customer-side review in low-leverage posture. The critical-issues-auditor correctly identified the absence of data-breach carve-outs as an existential issue in finding critical-issues-auditor-003, but that finding proposes TWO carve-outs (data breach + gross negligence) as a combined position. This finding (risk-allocation-analyst-001) and risk-allocation-analyst-002 provide the COMPONENT edits that implement the existential fix. Both should be restored. The super-cap limits Lattice's exposure to a defined multiple (3x) rather than uncapped liability, which is proportionate to the regulatory and remediation risks Customer faces in a data breach. This is a standard buyer-side position and does not constitute overreach.
restored_findings.position: Super-cap for data breaches at 3x trailing 12-month fees or insurance coverage limits, whichever is higher.
restored_findings.fallback: Super-cap at 2x trailing 12-month fees, applicable only to Security Incidents involving personal data where Lattice is determined to have breached its obligations under Section 5.2 or 5.3.
restored_findings.jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1). Assumed typical U.S. state data breach notification statutes apply.
restored_findings.suggested_text: Add to Section 8.6 exceptions: '(D) LATTICE'S LIABILITY FOR ANY SECURITY INCIDENT UNDER SECTION 5.3 INVOLVING UNAUTHORIZED ACCESS TO OR ACQUISITION OF CUSTOMER DATA CONTAINING PERSONAL DATA, PROVIDED THAT SUCH LIABILITY SHALL NOT EXCEED THREE (3) TIMES THE FEES PAID OR PAYABLE BY CUSTOMER TO LATTICE UNDER THE APPLICABLE ORDER FORM IN THE TWELVE (12) MONTHS IMMEDIATELY PRECEDING THE SECURITY INCIDENT.'
